metadata.summary: Bottom-up cost estimation with three-point PERT per WBS package, fully-loaded labor rates, risk-driven contingency, and P80-based cost baseline for defensible project budgets.

content.title: Cost Estimation
content.summary: A bottom-up cost estimation process: decompose the WBS into work packages, apply three-point PERT estimation per package, load labor with a fully-loaded multiplier (1.3-2.0x depending on region and benefits), add tools and infrastructure costs, calculate risk-driven contingency (sum of probability x cost-exposure per open risk), separate contingency reserve from management reserve, and produce a cost baseline (BAC = Cost Baseline + Management Reserve). The agent is a calculator and auditor, not a decision-maker; humans approve all numbers before they enter the budget.

why.p: Projects fail financially because they underestimate true costs, forget indirect costs, use flat-percentage contingencies that ignore actual risk exposure, and do not update the baseline when scope changes. Risk-driven contingency calibrates to actual exposure. Tracking P80 rather than P50 means the budget is breached only 20% of the time rather than 50%. Citing sources on every rate prevents hallucinated benchmarks from corrupting the baseline.

list.item: Producing the initial budget from an approved WBS before kickoff
Bottom-up cost roll-up for an RFP response requiring a defensible cost baseline
Build-vs-buy / build-vs-SaaS decisions where opportunity cost matters
Updating the cost baseline after an approved change-control event
Solopreneur "true cost" analysis pricing own time at market rate
Agile teams that fund team capacity by time-box, not by feature — use team-month run-rate, not bottom-up sum
Pre-discovery with requirements <30% defined — output will have false precision; use ROM (±50%) instead
Already-negotiated fixed-fee contracts — re-estimating internally has no contractual force
Tasks under 1 week where estimate cost exceeds work cost
Direct Costs — salaries, contractors, software licenses, hardware directly attributed to the project
Indirect Costs — office overhead, utilities, management overhead loaded as a percentage of direct labor
Fixed Costs — subscriptions, base salaries, regardless of project activity level
Variable Costs — overtime, cloud usage, contractor hours that scale with project work

section.name: bottom-up-estimation
section.title: Step 1: Bottom-Up Estimation
section.p: For each WBS work package, estimate labor hours by role and multiply by fully-loaded rate. Example: Design (16h x $75) + Development (40h x $100) + Testing (8h x $60) + Documentation (4h x $50) = $5,880 per package. Sum all packages for total direct labor cost.

list.item: Full-time employee: (annual salary x fully-loaded multiplier) / working days. Multiplier: 1.3x (low-tax market) to 2.0x (high-benefit market).
Contractor: hourly rate x estimated hours. Cite contract or market survey as source.
Software/SaaS: license + seats + integration cost over project duration.
Infrastructure: provisioned capacity x unit rate x duration; use infracost or cloud provider pricing API for accuracy.

section.name: three-point-pert
section.title: Step 3: Three-Point PERT
section.p: For uncertain cost items: Expected Cost = (Optimistic + 4 x Most Likely + Pessimistic) / 6. Example — cloud hosting: O=$200/mo, M=$350/mo, P=$800/mo → Expected = (200 + 1400 + 800)/6 = $400/mo. Track P80 of Monte Carlo simulation as the budget target, not P50.

section.name: risk-driven-contingency
section.title: Step 4: Risk-Driven Contingency
section.p: Sum (probability x cost_impact) for all open risks in the risk register. This replaces flat-percentage reserves, which systematically over- or under-fund based on risk profile. Unknown Unknowns (Management Reserve) is an additional 5-10% held by sponsors, released only for major scope changes.

list.item: Cost Baseline = Direct Costs + Indirect Costs + Risk-Driven Contingency Reserve
Budget at Completion (BAC) = Cost Baseline + Management Reserve
Lock the baseline after approval; re-baseline only on approved change requests
Track Schedule Performance Index (SPI) and Cost Performance Index (CPI) from EVM weekly

rule.statement: Cite source on every labor rate; reject any rate card that lacks a contract reference or market survey citation.
rule.rationale: LLMs hallucinate benchmark rates from stale training data; rates can differ 30%+ from current market; sourced rates are auditable and defensible.

rule.statement: Use risk-driven contingency (sum of probability x cost_impact per open risk); do not use a flat percentage when an active risk register exists.
rule.rationale: Flat contingency double-charges when combined with risk-driven reserve; it also ignores actual risk profile, over-funding low-risk and under-funding high-risk projects.

rule.statement: Track P80 of Monte Carlo simulation as the cost target, not P50 or PERT mean.
rule.rationale: P50 means the budget is breached 50% of the time; P80 provides a realistic target with acceptable overrun probability.

rule.statement: Separate management reserve from contingency reserve; contingency releases on identified risks; management reserve releases on scope changes only.
rule.rationale: Conflating the two removes sponsor control and misrepresents budget authority.

rule.statement: Effort hours are not elapsed days; model resource availability when computing duration-based costs (a 40-hour task for a 60%-available person costs 100% of their rate for 66 hours elapsed, not 40).
rule.rationale: Effort-elapsed confusion is the most common single source of budget overrun in agent-generated cost estimates.

antipattern.description: LLM outputs suspiciously round numbers ($50k, $100k, $200k) without showing the calculation.
antipattern.reason: Round numbers are anchors from training data, not estimates; require the bottom-up calculation or reject the output.

antipattern.description: Using a single overhead multiplier (1.3x) globally across all regions and employment types.
antipattern.reason: Overhead loading varies by region, tax regime, and benefits structure (1.3x to 2.0x); a single multiplier is wrong for cross-border projects.

antipattern.description: Including both risk-driven contingency AND a flat 15% on top of it.
antipattern.reason: Double-charging inflates the budget and misrepresents the risk exposure; pick one method and document the choice.

antipattern.description: Re-estimating after work has started without accounting for sunk costs, anchoring on the original number.
antipattern.reason: Re-estimates must derive from remaining scope only; anchoring to the original produces estimates that are always "a little over" the baseline.

section.name: agent-workflow
section.title: Agentic Workflow
section.p: The agent is a calculator + auditor, not a decision-maker. Feed it the WBS (work packages with effort hours), a labor rate card with sources, an infra/tools list, and a risk register; it produces a structured cost baseline (direct, indirect, contingency, management reserve) plus a Monte Carlo sensitivity. A human approves the numbers before they enter the budget. Re-run on every approved change request to keep the baseline live.

list.item: cost-estimator — reads wbs.yaml + rates.yaml, outputs cost-baseline JSON with PERT 3-point per work package
sensitivity-analyzer — Monte Carlo over the 3-point distributions, returns P50/P80/P95 totals with cost-driver tornado
risk-driven-reserve — converts risk register entries (probability × impact × cost exposure) into a risk-driven contingency, replacing flat %
faion-sdd-executor — drives implementation through quality gates once estimates become tasks
wbs.yaml: [{id, name, effort_optimistic_h, most_likely_h, pessimistic_h, role}]
rates.yaml: hourly rate per role, with cite source field
overhead: 1.3 (fully-loaded multiplier on labor)
risks.yaml: risk register with cost exposure per risk
duration_months: <int>
tools.yaml: SaaS subscriptions in scope

section.p: Output JSON should include: direct_costs_usd, indirect_costs_usd, risk_driven_contingency_usd, management_reserve_usd, cost_baseline_usd, budget_at_completion_usd, sensitivity (p50, p80, p95, tornado), and assumptions.

list.item: No invented rates; rates.yaml must include source per role
Round to nearest USD; never $X.XX precision
If any input missing, return {"error": "..."} not a guess
infracost — Cloud infra cost from Terraform / Pulumi diff (https://www.infracost.io/)
numpy + scipy.stats — Beta / triangular / lognormal distributions for PERT (https://scipy.org/)
montecarlo-cli (mcli) — Quick CLI Monte Carlo on cost ranges (https://github.com/cetra3/mcli)
gh / jira-cli — Pull effort / story-point data for bottom-up (https://cli.github.com/)
awscost-cli / gcloud billing / az consumption — Live cloud cost benchmarking
xlsxwriter / openpyxl — Generate cost worksheets in Excel format
currency-converter (cnvrt) — FX rates for multi-region budgets (OpenExchangeRates / ECB API)
Float / Resource Guru — SaaS, REST API. Capacity + rate cards; CSV export
Harvest / Toggl — SaaS, REST API. Actual hours by role for re-baselining
Forecast (Harvest) — SaaS, REST API. Project budget vs actuals
Productive.io — SaaS, REST API. Agency-grade cost + margin
Mosaic / Runn — SaaS, REST API. Resource forecasting
Infracost — SaaS / OSS. Infra cost baseline + PR diff
Vantage / CloudZero — SaaS, REST API. Cloud unit cost (cost per customer / per request)
Notion / Confluence — SaaS, REST API. Where the cost baseline doc lives
Excel / Google Sheets API — SaaS, REST API. Cost worksheet generation
QuickBooks / Xero API — SaaS, REST API. Actuals reconciliation
Cite source on every rate. "Senior engineer = $X" without a source is hallucinated; require the rate card to point to a contract / market survey.
Use risk-driven contingency, not flat 15%. Sum (probability × cost) of register entries; if list is empty, fix the risk process before the budget.
Separate management reserve from contingency. Contingency releases on identified risks; management reserve releases on scope changes only.
Track P80, not P50, in the baseline. P50 means you blow the budget half the time.
Re-baseline only on approved change requests. Constant re-baselining hides slip; lock the original.
Compare estimate to actual quarterly; feed estimation error back into the rate card and effort multipliers (calibration).
Rate hallucination: agents emit "$120/hr senior engineer" with no source. Always require rates.yaml as input, never let the model invent.
Burden multiplier confusion: salary × 1.3 ≠ cost. Add taxes, benefits, equipment, training, idle time; "fully-loaded" can be 1.4-2.0x.
Currency: agents output USD by default and silently convert; budgets must declare currency per line.
Contingency double-count: risk-driven reserve PLUS a flat 15% double-charges; pick one.
Stale benchmark prices: cloud, SaaS subscription, and contractor rates from training data can be 30% off; require live API fetch for high-stakes estimates.
Effort-vs-elapsed: 40 effort hours is not 5 days when developer is at 60% utilisation; force the agent to multiply by availability.
LLMs prefer round numbers (5, 10, 15, 25); round numbers are anchors, not estimates. Reject suspiciously round outputs.
Three-point estimation gives false precision: PERT mean assumes a beta distribution rarely true in software; pessimistic cost is consistently under-estimated by humans and LLMs.
Contingency reserve sized as a flat 10-25% ignores the risk register; high-risk projects need risk-driven contingency, not a percentage.
Overhead loading varies by region (1.3x in low-tax markets, 1.8x+ in high-tax / high-benefit markets); a single multiplier is wrong cross-border.
LLM benchmark hourly rates come from training data 12-24 months stale; current rates can differ by 30%+.
Currency / FX volatility in multi-region projects is not captured by static numbers.
Sunk-cost bias: re-estimates after work has started anchor on the original number rather than re-deriving from remaining scope.
Cost-loaded WBS hides resource calendar effects — a $100k labour line at 50% utilisation actually costs $200k of elapsed budget.

template.purpose: Full cost estimation table: direct / indirect / reserves / total budget

script.purpose: Risk-driven contingency via Monte Carlo over Bernoulli risk events; outputs expected, P50, P80, P95

see-also.link: PMI PMBOK Guide 6th Ed., Chapter 7 — Project Cost Management
AACE International Recommended Practice 18R-97 (Cost Estimate Classification)
Hubbard, How to Measure Anything (2014) — calibrated estimation
McConnell, Software Estimation: Demystifying the Black Art (2006)
COCOMO II model for parametric software estimation
Goldratt, The Goal — buffer placement vs per-task padding
